goals: Give the player a first-run onboarding flow to define their game universe before entering the chat.
Connect `assistant-ui` to the backend WebSocket so narrative responses stream in real time.
Display scene images (sent by the agent via the WebSocket) in a dedicated area alongside the chat.
Adhere strictly to all project standards: TypeScript strict mode, functional React components, ESLint + Prettier, no `console.log` in production paths, Vitest for unit tests.

userStories.title: Universe Onboarding Screen
userStories.description: As a player, I want to be greeted by a full-screen onboarding form where I describe my game universe so that the agent can build and narrate a world tailored to my setting before the chat begins.

acceptanceCriteria.text: A dedicated `/` route (or initial app state) shows a full-screen onboarding view with a textarea and a "Begin Adventure" submit button.
The textarea is focused on mount and accepts multi-line input.
Submitting with an empty textarea is blocked; a visible validation message is shown.
On submit, the setting text is sent to the backend as the first player message, and the app transitions to the chat view.
Typecheck (`tsc --noEmit`) and lint (`eslint`) pass with no errors.
Visually verified in browser: onboarding screen renders, validation fires, submission navigates to chat.

userStories.title: Streamed Narrative Chat
userStories.description: As a player, I want to type messages in a chat interface and see the agent's narrative response appear word-by-word as it streams so that the game feels alive and responsive. > Implementation Note: Before implementing the `WebSocketRuntime`, fetch and read > https://www.assistant-ui.com/docs to confirm the correct `useAssistantRuntime` interface > and custom runtime contract. Do not infer the API from training data — `assistant-ui` > evolves frequently.

acceptanceCriteria.text: `assistant-ui` chat component is mounted on the chat view (`/chat` route or app state).
Player messages are sent to the backend over WebSocket.
Agent responses stream token-by-token into the message bubble using `assistant-ui`'s streaming API.
A typing/loading indicator is visible while the agent is generating.
Completed messages are retained in the conversation history for the session.
Typecheck and lint pass with no errors.
Visually verified in browser: message sends, streaming text appears incrementally, full message persists.

userStories.title: Scene Image Display
userStories.description: As a player, I want a dedicated area next to the chat that shows the latest scene image generated by the agent so that I can visualize the world being narrated.

acceptanceCriteria.text: A scene image panel (right panel on desktop layout) is rendered alongside the chat.
When the backend WebSocket sends a `scene_image` event (base64 or URL), the panel updates with the new image.
While no image has arrived yet, the panel shows a placeholder (dark background with a subtle "Awaiting scene…" label).
Image transitions use a CSS crossfade (opacity transition) between the old and new image.
The panel is responsive: on narrow viewports (< 768 px) the image collapses below the chat.
Typecheck and lint pass with no errors.
Visually verified in browser: placeholder renders on load, new image replaces placeholder with crossfade, responsive layout is correct.

userStories.title: WebSocket Connection Status Indicator
userStories.description: As a player, I want to see a visible indicator of the WebSocket connection state so that I know when the game is connected and when it is not.

acceptanceCriteria.text: A small status badge (e.g. green dot = connected, red dot = disconnected) is always visible in the UI.
When the WebSocket closes unexpectedly, the badge updates and a non-blocking toast/banner informs the player.
The client attempts a single automatic reconnect after a 3-second delay on unexpected disconnect.
Typecheck and lint pass with no errors.
Visually verified in browser: disconnect simulation (DevTools → offline) shows the indicator change and reconnect attempt.

functionalRequirements.description: ** The frontend is a Vite + React 18 + TypeScript SPA living at `apps/frontend`, following all conventions in `PROJECT_CONTEXT.md`.

** `assistant-ui` is used as the chat component via a custom `WebSocketRuntime` built with `useAssistantRuntime`; no REST adapter and no custom chat bubble implementation from scratch. Before implementing, fetch and read https://www.assistant-ui.com/docs to verify the current API contract — do not infer it from training data.
** The WebSocket client connects to `VITE_BACKEND_WS_URL` (from `.env`); the URL must be configurable via environment variable.
** WebSocket messages follow a typed discriminated union: `{ type: "token", content: string }` for streamed text and `{ type: "scene_image", url: string }` for scene images.
** All React components are functional components with TypeScript types; no `any`.
** The app has at least one Vitest unit test per new custom hook introduced (e.g. `useWebSocket`).
** Structured logging replaces `console.log`; production paths must not contain raw `console.log` calls.
** The layout on desktop is a two-panel design: chat panel (left, ~60% width) + scene image panel (right, ~40% width).

** The WebSocket message discriminated union (`FR-4`) is defined once in `/packages/shared-types` and imported by both `apps/frontend` and the backend service; local copies in individual workspaces are not allowed.
